Data Representation.title: Data Representation
Data Representation.content: Computers store text, images, sound, and numbers as binary patterns. Data representation covers ASCII, Unicode, binary arithmetic, and the different ways to encode information.

Decimal number system.title: Decimal Number System
Decimal number system.content: The denary number system, also known as the decimal system or base-10 system, is the most commonly used number system in everyday life. It consists of ten digits, ranging from 0 to 9. Examples of denary numbers include 458, 2398, 9870, and 1254. This system forms the foundation of our daily numerical calculations and is the most intuitive for human understanding.

Binary number system.title: Binary Number System
Binary number system.content: The binary number system, also referred to as base-2, forms the fundamental building block of all computer systems. It consists of only two digits: 0 and 1. Computers contain millions of switches that can only exist in one of two states: ON or OFF. In binary representation, 1 represents an ON state while 0 represents an OFF state. Examples of binary numbers include 10, 101, 11011, and 111000001.

Hexadecimal System.title: Hexadecimal System
Hexadecimal System.content: The hexadecimal system is a base-16 number system that provides a more human-friendly way to represent binary data. One hexadecimal digit replaces four binary digits, making complex binary numbers significantly more compact. For instance, the binary number 1101001010101111 can be represented as D2AF in hexadecimal.

Two's Complement.title: Two's Complement
Two's Complement.content: Two's complement is the method employed by computers to represent negative binary numbers. Since computers cannot process negative signs, this special system enables the representation of negative values.

ASCII.title: ASCII
ASCII.content: ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange) is a character encoding standard that supports English characters. The extended ASCII version provides additional characters, but the standard remains limited to English alphabets.

Unicode.title: Unicode
Unicode.content: Unicode emerged as a universal character set that includes virtually every language in the world. This system uses up to 4 bytes (32 bits) per character and can store thousands of characters. Notably, the first 128 characters of Unicode are exactly identical to ASCII, ensuring backward compatibility. Unicode supports diverse languages including English, Russian, Chinese, and Arabic, making it the preferred character encoding system for modern computing.

Images.title: Images
Images.content: Computers utilize two types of images: bitmap and vector. Bitmap images consist of pixels (picture elements), where each pixel stores colour information. This pixel-based representation forms the foundation of most digital photography and raster graphics.

Data Storage.title: Data Storage
Data Storage.content: A bit represents the basic unit of computing memory storage, containing either a 1 or 0. One byte consists of 8 bits, while a 4-bit number is called a nibble. Storage capacity is measured using multiples of bytes, with prefixes such as kilo, mega, and giga. The binary-based system uses powers of 2 rather than the decimal-based SI system.

Lossy Compression.title: Lossy Compression
Lossy Compression.content: Lossy compression removes unnecessary data from a file, resulting in a significantly smaller file size at the cost of permanent quality loss. The original file cannot be reconstructed after lossy compression.

Lossless Compression.title: Lossless Compression
Lossless Compression.content: Lossless compression preserves all data without any loss of detail, making it suitable for situations where data integrity is critical. Run Length Encoding (RLE) is a lossless compression method that finds repeated identical data and represents it more efficiently.
